text: In this video, we attempt to describe the stepwise protocol of performing a type A radical hysterectomy with pelvic lymph node dissection for a case of cancer endometrium stage Ia that is endometrioid grade II. We also attempt to discuss the need and the utility of the new Querlu and Moro classification and its advantages over the past various classifications of radical hysterectomy. We will therefore take a few minutes to recapitulate this new classification and its anatomical basis so that we understand the surgery well. This is also followed by a systematic pelvic lymphadenectomy which is also discussed taking its anatomical limits and landmarks. So we have the Coelho and the Moro classification which was accepted in the year two thousand and eight. Through this a worldwide standard was given. Basically the new classification came up with the realization that the Pivar-Rutledge classification did not have fixed anatomical landmarks which serve as a basis for demarcating the extent of radicality. As a result, two surgeons performing the same type III radical procedure would end up with different specimen limits. Because of a lack of clarity, there was a different interpretation in each one's concept. Also, we saw that the old classification did not take into account the laparoscopic or the minimally invasive approach and also did not take into account the nerve sparing approach and procedures like radical trachelectomy all of which have a place in modern oncology. Coming to the classification, we will now see that it has four main classes which are based on very well defined anatomical landmarks. We also note that the term paracervix or paracervical lymphadenectomy is added. So what are the landmarks of this new classification? Basically we have four classes which are based on three anatomical landmarks. These are the ureter, the uterine artery, at its origin and the pelvic side wall. On the basis of these three anatomical structures radical hysterectomy has been divided into four major groups. So this picture shows us a depiction of these three landmarks. The first structure is the ureter which is pointed by the green arrow and is also marked in green and we see it coursing through the pararectal fossa. So the second landmark is the uterine artery as it emerges out of the anterior division of the internal iliac. We notice this from the red dot shown in this picture. Also notice that the other branch is the umbilical artery as it goes superiorly.

The third landmark shown in yellow is the pelvic side wall also marked by the yellow asterisk here. So interestingly these three landmarks may also serve or may also be described as levels of surgical plane from medial to lateral, level one being the ureter, level two being the divisions of the anterior division of the internal iliac and level three being the external iliac and the pelvic sidewall. So basically if you consider any radical hysterectomy There are four things which are taken into account when describing the radicality of that surgery. The first and the most important is the level of resection of the paracervix or which was also known as the parametra. The second is the level at which the bladder and the rectal pillars have been taken. The third is the vaginal margin that has been taken and the fourth is the What is the overall goal of the procedure? Basically simply dividing the Paracervix resection into four types, this classification has four main groups. It is type A, B, C and D as is shown in this present picture. Notice that type A resects the paracervix at a level which is between the cervix and the ureter also shown by the green line here. In type B radical hysterectomy shown by the colour yellow here we actually unroof the ureter and lateralize it at the level of the ureteric tunnel. This is shown by the yellow line in this picture. In type C radical hysterectomy shown by the colour blue here, the parametra is taken at the level of the origin of the uterine artery from the anterior division of the internal iliac. In type D resection the parametra is taken almost to the level of the pelvic side wall exposing till the sciatic nerve root. So if you see the diagram here we see the extending limits of the parametra laterally from type A to type B further going laterally in type C at the division of the internal iliac and in type D reaching as far as the pelvic side wall. So these are fixed anatomical landmarks which help us define our lateral paracervical dissection. A detailed description of all the four types of classes of the radical hysterectomy is beyond the scope of this video. Therefore, we will just describe type A here and this is what we will see in the subsequent surgery.

The surgeon begins the type A radical hysterectomy by placing a primary umbilical 10 mm optical trocar and four secondary working ports. The surgical team stands on the left side of the patient, using ipsilateral ports. The dissection starts with opening the peritoneum below the right infundibulopelvic ligament, developing the rectovaginal space in the midline using a harmonic scalpel for precise dissection. The surgeon carefully opens the two layers of the Denonvilliers fascia, revealing the rectovaginal space. On the left side, the dissection of the left pararectal space is initiated, tracing the ureter and the division of the internal iliac vascular system. The uterine artery is identified at its origin and ligated using the harmonic scalpel's three-clip method. The deep uterine vein is noted at a deeper level, important for nerve-sparing procedures. The peritoneum is further opened to enlarge the spaces, with tenting movements of the Maryland grasper aiding in precise dissection.

The surgeon further develops the right pararectal space, then opens the round ligament near the pelvic side wall to access the retroperitoneum. The round ligament is cut parallel to the right infundibulopelvic ligament, which is skeletonized. A similar procedure is performed on the left side. The vesico-vaginal space is developed by extending the peritoneum cut anteriorly and lifting the fold to push the bladder caudally. The bladder pillar is taken at the cervix level. The infundibulopelvic ligament is ligated using a vessel sealing device, and the uterine artery is divided at the internal os.

The surgeon performs an extra facial hysterectomy without uterine manipulation, with assistant graspers holding the uterus. The para cervix is resected midway between the cervix and ureter, marked by an oblique red line. The vagina is opened, and a small three to five millimeter rim of vaginal tissue is excised around the cervix, maintaining a margin of less than one centimeter. The cervix is kept in view, and cuts are made into the vagina to include the entire cervix in the specimen. The procedure transitions to pelvic lymph node dissection, with the peritoneum fold hitched anteriorly to the abdominal wall for better lateral exposure.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the pelvic lymph nodes at the lateral border of the external iliac vascular system using a vessel sealing device. Small bursts of energy are applied to meticulously expose the external iliac vein, while the assistant grasper protects the vein laterally. The dissection is performed within defined limits: laterally by the genitofemoral nerve on the psoas muscle, medially by the ureter, inferiorly by the obturator nerve, caudally by the corona mortis, and superiorly by the bifurcation of the common iliac artery. The external iliac vein is bared, and the surgeon moves towards the ilio-obturator group of lymph nodes, ensuring to stay above the obturator nerve.

The surgeon identifies an enlarged node within the external iliac group of lymph nodes on the left side and carefully dissects around it to preserve the nodal tissue. The dissection proceeds towards the ilio obturator group of lymph nodes, with the obturator nerve visible at a deep level marked by a yellow arrow. The deep circumflex iliac vein, marked by oblique blue lines, is observed communicating between the external iliac vein and the obturator vein, forming the corona mortis. The assistant applies traction to the nodal mass, facilitating the division of lymphatics.

The surgeon closes the vaginal vault using a number one vicryl suture on a round body needle, employing an ipsilateral suturing technique. The nodal masses are placed into an endobag and extracted vaginally. The final view of the pelvis is shown after completing the pelvic lymphadenectomy.
